common.error_generic: Something went wrong. Please try again.

language_select.welcome_en: Welcome — choose your language to continue.
language_select.welcome_tl: Maligayang pagdating — pumili ng wika para magpatuloy.
language_select.note: You can change this anytime in Settings.

onboarding.intro_body: This takes about 5 minutes. Your answers help us build a daily routine and goals made just for your family.
onboarding.save_later_confirm: Your progress has been saved. You can continue anytime from the home screen.

step_child_name.question: What is your child's name?
step_child_name.helper: This is how we'll refer to them throughout the app.

step_child_age.question: How old is {{name}}?
step_child_age.helper: Age helps us suggest age-appropriate activities and routines.

step_diagnosis.question: When was {{name}} diagnosed?
step_diagnosis.helper: Approximate year is fine. This helps us understand where you are in your journey.

step_communication.question: How does {{name}} communicate?
step_communication.helper: Choose the option that best describes them right now.
step_communication.fluent_desc: Speaks clearly most of the time

step_challenges.question: What are {{name}}'s biggest challenges right now?
step_challenges.helper: Select all that apply — be honest, this is just for you.

step_strengths.question: What does {{name}} love or do well?
step_strengths.helper: Select all that apply. We build routines around strengths.

step_goals.question: What are your top goals for {{name}} right now?
step_goals.helper: Pick up to 3. We'll build a plan around these.

step_support.question: Who else supports {{name}}?
step_support.helper: This helps us acknowledge your whole care team.

onboarding.result_subtitle: Here's {{name}}'s personalised daily routine and your 3 parent goals.

why.predictable_start: A consistent start helps regulate your child's nervous system and reduces morning anxiety.
why.hygiene_routine: Step-by-step hygiene routines build independence and sensory tolerance over time.
why.structured_meal: Eating at consistent times reduces mealtime refusal and builds nutritional security.
why.visual_schedule: Seeing the day ahead reduces anxiety and helps your child feel safe and in control.
why.sensory_warmup: Gentle sensory input before school prepares the nervous system and improves focus.
why.aac_morning: Morning AAC practice sets the tone for communication throughout the day.
why.calm_transition: Transition warnings and visual cues reduce the stress of moving between activities.
why.midday_rest: Short rest breaks prevent sensory overload and support sustained attention.
why.movement_break: Movement regulates the nervous system and improves focus for learning.
why.learning_activity: Predictable learning time builds academic routine without pressure.
why.therapy_session: Consistent therapy scheduling helps skills generalise into daily life.
why.afternoon_play: Child-led free play builds creativity, self-regulation, and joy.
why.sensory_break: Scheduled sensory breaks prevent cumulative overload before meltdown.
why.social_play: Low-pressure social time builds connection skills without the demands of school.
why.calming_dinner: A calm dinner environment supports digestion and family connection.
why.bath_routine: A warm bath with consistent steps signals the body that sleep is coming.
why.wind_down: Screen-free wind-down time helps transition the nervous system to sleep mode.
why.story_time: Shared reading builds language, bonding, and a positive sleep association.
why.preview_tomorrow: Previewing tomorrow reduces overnight anxiety and morning resistance.
why.sleep_hygiene: Consistent sleep timing improves mood, learning, and behaviour across the board.

tips.morning_wake_nonverbal: Use a consistent sound or light cue (same song, same lamp) rather than words.
tips.morning_wake_verbal: Give a 5-minute warning before asking them to get up. Rushing causes dysregulation.
tips.visual_schedule_tip: Point to each picture card as you complete it, then let your child move or tick it off.
tips.hygiene_steps: Break hygiene into 3–5 picture steps posted in the bathroom. Praise each step.
tips.breakfast_picky: Offer one safe food alongside one new food. Never force — exposure builds tolerance.
tips.sensory_warmup_tip: Try 2 minutes of heavy work (pushing against a wall, carrying a backpack) before school.
tips.aac_morning_tip: Point to the schedule picture on the AAC board and say the word together each morning.
tips.calm_transition: Give a 5-minute, then 2-minute verbal and visual warning before every transition.
tips.midday_rest_tip: A quiet corner with headphones, a weighted blanket, or dim light works well.
tips.movement_tip: Star jumps, animal walks, or a short walk around the block are all effective.
tips.afternoon_play_tip: Let your child choose the activity. Unstructured time reduces demand and builds joy.
tips.sensory_break_tip: Watch for early signs of overload (covering ears, pacing) and offer the break proactively.
tips.social_tip: Start with one familiar peer for 20 minutes rather than group settings.
tips.bath_tip: Use the same bath products, same order, same towel. Predictability is calming.
tips.wind_down_tip: Dim lights 30 minutes before bed. Avoid screens. Soft music or white noise helps.
tips.story_tip: Let your child choose the book. Re-reading the same book is fine — it's comforting.
tips.preview_tip: Use 2–3 picture cards to show what happens tomorrow morning. Keep it brief and positive.
tips.sleep_tip: A consistent bedtime within 15 minutes each night has the biggest impact on sleep quality.

improve_sleep_routine.title: Build a consistent bedtime routine
improve_sleep_routine.behavior: Complete a 4-step wind-down routine (bath, dim lights, story, sleep) at the same time each night
improve_sleep_routine.why: Sleep is the single biggest lever for behaviour, focus, and mood. A predictable bedtime routine trains the brain to expect sleep.
improve_sleep_routine.how: Start with just one change — pick a consistent bedtime and stick to it for 7 nights before adding more steps.
improve_sleep_routine.week2: 2-step routine (bath + story) completed 5 out of 7 nights
improve_sleep_routine.week4: Full 4-step routine completed most nights; your child shows fewer bedtime resistance behaviours

consistent_mealtime.title: Create calm, structured mealtimes
consistent_mealtime.behavior: Serve meals at the same time each day with a consistent plate, seating, and visual sequence
consistent_mealtime.why: Predictable mealtimes reduce sensory stress around food and gradually expand food acceptance.
consistent_mealtime.how: Pick one meal to start (usually breakfast). Use the same plate, same seat, same 2–3 foods for one week.
consistent_mealtime.week1: Breakfast served at the same time with no pressure for 7 days
consistent_mealtime.week2: Your child sits for 10+ minutes at 2 meals per day without major distress
consistent_mealtime.week4: Mealtime refusal drops noticeably; your child tolerates one new food alongside safe foods

reduce_meltdowns.title: Build a calming toolkit for big moments
reduce_meltdowns.behavior: Identify 3 early warning signs and practise 2 calming strategies before meltdown escalates
reduce_meltdowns.why: Meltdowns are communication. Teaching regulation tools helps your child self-soothe and reduces family stress.
reduce_meltdowns.how: This week: identify your child's 3 earliest warning signs. Write them down.
reduce_meltdowns.week1: You can name 3 early signs and 1 effective calming strategy
reduce_meltdowns.week2: You intervene at the early sign stage at least 3 times without escalation
reduce_meltdowns.week4: Meltdown duration and frequency visibly reduced; your child begins using 1 strategy themselves

communication_practice.title: Daily communication practice
communication_practice.behavior: Spend 10 minutes each day in a focused communication exchange — verbal, AAC, or gesture-based
communication_practice.why: Frequent, low-pressure communication opportunities are the most effective way to build language — more than formal therapy alone.
communication_practice.how: Follow your child's lead. Narrate what they are doing ('You're pushing the car!') without requiring a response.
communication_practice.week1: 10-minute play-based communication session happens 5 out of 7 days
communication_practice.week2: Your child initiates communication (gesture, sound, word) at least once per session
communication_practice.week4: Noticeable increase in communication attempts during the session and in daily life

toileting_independence.title: Build toileting independence step by step
toileting_independence.behavior: Follow a consistent toilet schedule and visual toilet routine
toileting_independence.why: Toileting independence improves self-confidence and reduces one of the biggest sources of family stress.
toileting_independence.how: Start with scheduled toilet trips every 2 hours. Use a simple visual sequence posted in the bathroom.
toileting_independence.week1: Scheduled toilet trips happen consistently without refusal most days
toileting_independence.week2: Your child completes 2+ steps of the visual routine independently
toileting_independence.week4: Your child initiates or cooperates with toileting at least 70% of the time

sensory_regulation.title: Develop a daily sensory diet
sensory_regulation.behavior: Provide 3 scheduled sensory input opportunities throughout the day
sensory_regulation.why: A predictable sensory diet reduces overload, improves focus, and gives your child's nervous system what it needs to stay regulated.
sensory_regulation.how: Identify your child's top sensory need (movement? pressure? quiet?). Add one 10-minute sensory break after breakfast.
sensory_regulation.week1: One morning sensory break added to the routine with no resistance
sensory_regulation.week2: Three sensory breaks scheduled; your child transitions more smoothly to activities afterwards
sensory_regulation.week4: Fewer sensory-driven meltdowns; your child begins to seek their sensory tools independently

social_skills.title: Create regular low-pressure social moments
social_skills.behavior: Arrange one structured social activity per week with a familiar peer
social_skills.why: Repeated, low-pressure social experiences build connection skills far more effectively than group settings.
social_skills.how: Start with a 20-minute playdate at home (your child's safe space) with one familiar child.
social_skills.week1: One playdate or social activity completed — any outcome is success
social_skills.week2: Your child tolerates 20+ minutes of proximity with another child without major distress
social_skills.week4: Your child shows interest in or initiates interaction with a peer at least once

school_readiness.title: Build a strong school-to-home transition
school_readiness.behavior: Create a consistent after-school decompression routine
school_readiness.why: Children with autism often hold it together at school and decompress at home. A predictable landing routine prevents meltdowns and eases re-entry.
school_readiness.how: After school: offer a silent snack, no demands, and preferred activity for 15–20 minutes before homework or tasks.
school_readiness.week1: After-school snack routine in place with less than 3 meltdowns this week
school_readiness.week2: Your child transitions from school to home and decompresses within 20 minutes most days
school_readiness.week4: School day emotional regulation has improved; fewer end-of-day meltdowns

following_instructions.title: Practice following 1–2 step instructions
following_instructions.behavior: Give one clear instruction at a time using their name, a pause, and one action
following_instructions.why: Simple, clear instructions reduce processing demands and build cooperation without confrontation.
following_instructions.how: This week: use the Name-Pause-Instruction format ('Aiden… [pause] … shoes on'). Wait 5 seconds. Prompt once.
following_instructions.week1: You consistently use Name-Pause-Instruction format; your child follows 1-step instructions 50%+ of the time
following_instructions.week2: Your child follows familiar 1-step instructions with first prompt most of the time
following_instructions.week4: Cooperation rate at home has increased noticeably; fewer refusals to simple requests

emotional_regulation.title: Build an emotional vocabulary together
emotional_regulation.behavior: Name emotions aloud during the day and offer a calm-down tool when regulation is hard
emotional_regulation.why: Children who can name their feelings regulate them better. Modelling this yourself is the most powerful teaching.
emotional_regulation.how: Start by naming your own emotions out loud ('I feel frustrated right now — I'm going to take a deep breath').
emotional_regulation.week1: You name emotions (yours and theirs) at least 3× per day this week
emotional_regulation.week2: Your child begins to use at least one feeling word or gesture spontaneously
emotional_regulation.week4: Your child recognises 3+ emotions in themselves or others and uses one calming strategy

settings.onboarding_redo_confirm: This will take you back through the setup survey. Your current plan will be replaced.
